QF.id: Taxation of Debt and Equity Products_8

question.text: Deepa, an active trader, incurred a net loss of Rs.3,00,000 from equity futures and options trading during FY 2025-26. Her other income includes salary of Rs.12,00,000 and rental income of Rs.2,40,000. How should this F&O loss be treated for tax purposes?

answer.text: It is a speculative business loss and can only be set off against speculative business income
It is a capital loss and can be set off against capital gains only
It is a non-speculative business loss and can be set off against salary and rental income in the same year
It is a non-speculative business loss; it cannot be set off against salary income but can be carried forward for 8 assessment years to be set off against business income

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under Section 43(5), derivatives (futures and options) trading is specifically excluded from the definition of "speculative transaction." Therefore, F&O losses are classified as non-speculative business losses. Non-speculative business losses can be set off against any income EXCEPT salary income in the current year. However, if not fully set off, they can be carried forward for 8 assessment years and set off only against business income (not any other head). **The Trap:** Option A is the most common trap — many candidates think derivatives = speculation. The law explicitly excludes exchange-traded derivatives from speculative transactions. Option C is partially right (it IS non-speculative) but wrong on set-off — business losses CANNOT be set off against salary income. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series VIII (Equity Derivatives) heavily tests the non-speculative classification of F&O under Section 43(5). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Advise clients that F&O losses, while non-speculative, have limited set-off utility. They cannot offset salary or capital gains, making F&O trading losses potentially "stranded" for salaried individuals without other business income.
